entry.title: Where can I start learning to relate SAP BW bridge to SAP Datasphere?
entry.content:
We  recommend to access our Modeling in SAP Datasphere, SAP BW bridge learning journey

Overview
After completing this learning journey, you will be able to model and provision data in SAP Datasphere, SAP BW bridge.

Learning objectives
In this learning journey, you will learn to relate SAP BW bridge to SAP Datasphere, define transport settings, create BW objects, load data to BW Bridge, setting up a schedule for data loading, and consume BW Bridge objects.

Prerequisites 

  • Exploring SAP Datasphere

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area.

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you.

We appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in May
entry.content:
A few days before SAPPHIRE we close another awesome month packed with great 🚀 new features in SAP Datasphere. There are also lots of excellent new blogs in the community in May.

 

My top features in May

Multi-lingual metadata support

The multi-language support was just extended with the introduction of the translation dashboard where you are now able to also translate the object metadata, specifically for analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud. You can now access the dashboard and translate metadata such as business names and column names for dimensions and analytic models, and also hierarchy dimension labels for stories to different languages. You can translate manually or via an XLIFF file, but also manage already existing translations, and update them. For more information check out the online documentation and the blog SAP Datasphere Multilingual Support - Translation Dashboard.

Efficiency Features

We have added features in various areas of the product helping to use the product way more efficiently.

  1. In the analytic model you can now copy existing measures to create a new measure. Very helpful if you are creating a bunch of similar measures. In addition, for measures, attributes and associated dimensions there is now a Select All/Unselect All checkbox which is very helpful. More information.
  2. Data access controls are now listed in the Data Builder application and in addition to the relocation, you can now import and export data access controls in CSN/JSON files. More information.
  3. If email notification is set up for a task chain, you will now receive email notifications if an error occurs during initialization or preparation phase to run the task chain, before the task chain run actually starts. More information.
  4. In the data marketplace you can now duplicate existing data products which helps especially if you create lots of similar data products. More information.

 

 

My top blogs in May

Multi-language is not new to SAP Datasphere. The details of the new and existing features are described by @mona_durai in the
➡️ SAP Datasphere Multilingual Support - Master Data Translation and by @senthurshree in the blog
➡️ SAP Datasphere Multilingual Support - Translation Dashboard.

@GaetanSaulnier highlights the new catalog source to be able to harvest metadata from the SAP BW bridge in his great blog
➡️ SAP Datasphere catalog - Harvesting from SAP Datasphere, SAP BW bridge.

Now that Confluent Kafka is available as a target in transformation flows @DanielKraemer gets you all the details in his blog
➡️ Replication Flow Blog Part 6 – Confluent as Replication Target.

@Sefan_Linders blogs about how you can partition a transformation flow in SAP Datasphere by using the CLI
➡️ Transformation Flow partition generation with SAP Datasphere CLI.

 

More blogs to check out

  • ディメンション(ビジネスエンティティ)に階層ビューを外部階層として割り当ててテキストを表示する方法

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Getting Started, Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting and the FAQ for SAP Datasphere.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: What happens when a Data Flow fails?
entry.content:
You are using DSP as a Data Fabric to load multiple data sources, and all works great, and you can enjoy life (you know, teleworking from some heavenly place) ... until something goes wrong. You receive an email that a Data Flow has failed. That SFTP seems to be not as reliable as it should be, so now what?

Leave your Virgin Mojito and open DSP.

 

 

 

 

Test definition

The test consists of loading a CSV with 30,000 rows by means of a Data Flow, and to provoke an error in the middle of the process, to see what we obtain in the destination table.

The CSV has 30000 rows, with two fields:

  • ID: consecutive numeric
  • VAL: string with the value '1' in all rows except 20101 which has a 'AAA'.

The source is SFTP, configuring the CSV as:

  • ID as Int32
  • VAL as String(1).
  • Control Fetch Size active to 1000 rows.
  • Fail Run on String Truncate = On. This will cause row 20101 to give an error for exceeding the defined size of string(1).

The destination is a DSP local table with

  • ID: int32
  • VAL: String (1)
  • Control Fetch Size active to 1000 rows.

 

Test Results

The execution of the data flow ends with error.

In the destination table, data has been saved, specifically the IDs ranging from 1 to 19000.
So I draw two conclusions:
- The most important is that the DF execution is not an “all or nothing” type, and commits have been made during the process.
- The second conclusion is that as we have two fetch controls, in source and in destination, it seems that this makes that the block of rows 19,001-20,000 has not been loaded even if it did not have any error, surely, when the block of 1,000 rows with error was read the exception was thrown, and the part that saves in the destination had not yet processed the block of 19,001-20,000, and that is why we do not have it.

I did a second test by increasing the rows to 110,000, leaving the wrong row at 105,001, and removing the fetch size control on both sides, to check if the theoretical maximum limit of the fetch size is 100,000.
But it saved from row 1 to 104,000 ... implying that the fetch size was not 100,000. I don't know if the value it takes depends on the source, the combination of them, etc.

 

Which implies

When doing ETLs with delta loads that are manually managed based on e.g. timestamps, there is a risk that when an error occurs, rows from a failed load are left in the target table.
This may cause the calculation of the next timestamp to be loaded to be erroneous, so these rows should be deleted before their calculation.

entry.title: Best of Suite vs. Modern Data Stack
entry.content:
I just have seen a talk between Martin Kleppmann and Jesse Anderson about “Designing A Data-Intensive Future” from GOTO 2023 event. Martin Kleppmann and he said “When you get to large data workload, if you have a single tool that can do everything, the only way that can do that is by doing everything badly.“

I think this is a basic idea where the Modern Data Stack (MDS) evolve from. The idea of Modern Data Stack and how it evolved, is described in my former blog Data Architecture with SAP – Modern Data Stack. Over the time many characteristics where added what a MDS must archive:

 

Fig. 1: Characteristics of the Modern Data Stack

The idea was, to create a Best-of-Breed software architecture for data, which brought together the best fit of data capabilities needed to create maximum of value from data for the enterprise. But is this idea of having always the best tool doing this job is still valid?

While Best-of-Breed is not a new idea, we have seen the pendulum swings from Best-of-Breed to Best-of-Suite in the data world and back again. Currently even the end of the MDS was proclaimed. Why? Because over time several developments happend:

  • Customers became more price sensitive and data tools became standard in companies. Standards are goal for optimization so it is easier to manage licences, support, etc by one or at least a few reduces complexity and costs.

  • Differentiation between providers is increasingly disappearing. The evolution of cloud services is fast. From the capability perspective Best-of-Suite solutions like SAP and Oracle catched up. It is more about evolution speed, partnership and details now.

  • MDS is converging to a Best-of-Suite. I see that particularly for Google Cloud with BigQuery in the center of the stack where Google is able to offer a End-to-End approach and simliar for Databricks except the BI part. Snowflake is possibly still the most representative MDS data platform and really celebrating the ecosystem idea with strong partnerships. But they are also growing there own stack with innovations like Streamlit, Snowpipe and Snowpark.

  • Customers coming from one side shifting over time to a mixed scenario to adapt to the advantages of the other side. Means customer starting e. g. with Databricks on Microsoft Azure adapting more and more Azure services over time as the Suite approach making things easier (integration, less vendors, good enough services, …). Same If you start with a suite but extend for new BI tools, ETL/ELT and other components to complete or compensate.

  • Best-of-Suite vendors like SAP are embracing data ecosystems with partners like Collibra, DataRobot, Confluent and Databrick to close gaps and offer alternatives for a better adaption of the data stack.

  • Maybe a short term trend but something I see, is the shifted focus from data to AI with the ChatGPT/LLM hype. Maybe this pendelum will come back when people understand that data is the base for AI and even more for data-centric AI (compared to discriminative/generative AI).

  • Once I read, the biggest lie of a data architecture picture is that there is only one data platform in the middle. There is at least one old, one new, one from an acquisition, in germany at least you will find a extra one for HR. From a certain size or complexity companies today have several data platforms and redundant tools. It’s the new normal somehow.

The last aspect brings us to a new perspective. We need maybe best of both worlds because every world (Best-of-Suite / Best-of-Breed) have their advantaged (and disadvantages).

 

Fig 2: Combining characteristics of Best-of-Breed (MDS) and Best-of-Suite (here: SAP) 

So now if we think a little bit further, these worlds are converging into a open ecosystem which not only adapts to the best fit for creating value from data, but also adapt optimal to the operational ecosystem like SAP driven enterprises as a next level of ecosystems.

 Fig. 3: Convergence of MDS and Best-of-Suite vendors into a data ecosystem

We thougt deeply about this development in our latest whitepaper about SAP & Modern Data Stack available here (1) (in german only at the moment).

An extended excerpt can be found here with Snowflake and here with Databricks.

What do you think or experience where data stacks evolve? Is one vendor delivering everything for you and doing well? Do you build an ecosystem of tools to solve complex problems or is the MDS-approach still the best helping you in your daily data business?

-----------------------

This blog was initially posted on Substack.

----------------------- 

(1) https://www.infomotion.de/whitepaper-sap-modern-data-stack

entry.title: Securing Your Data with Data Access Controls in Datasphere
entry.content:
Datasphere, a comprehensive data management platform, offers a suite of tools and features to help organizations safeguard their data assets and ensure compliance with security regulations. In this blog post, I will demonstrate how to implement data access controls in Datasphere to enhance security and protect your valuable data.

 

 

Understanding Data Access Controls

Data access controls in Datasphere typically refer to the mechanisms and policies put in place to manage and restrict access to data within the Datasphere platform. These controls ensure that only authorized users or systems can access specific datasets or resources, helping to maintain data security, privacy, and compliance with regulations.

 

Business Proposal:

Business User Perspective

Anna is the Sales Manager, she understands the business needs and the sensitivity of sales data.

John is the Data Analyst from the Irish Sales Department, he uses sales data for analysis and reporting.

Anna and John have a meeting to discuss the upcoming implementation of SAP Datasphere. Anna is aware of the importance of data security and compliance with data governance policies. They decide they need to set up a permissions table to define who can access what data which can then be shared with the IT team so they can implement these controls in Datasphere.

 

Proposal for Permissions Access:

Sales Executives: Full access to all sales data.

Regional Sales Managers: Access restricted to their respective regions.

Sales Analysts: Access to anonymized or aggregated data for analysis purposes.

 

Technical User Perspective

Sarah is the IT & Security Administrator: She specializes in data modelling and security and is responsible for setting up and managing Datasphere.

Sarah receives the permissions table from Anna and John. She begins planning how to implement these controls in SAP Datasphere.

 

Creating Data Access Controls in Datasphere

Prerequisites

  • Before creating your data access control, you must have prepared a permissions entity with the following columns:
    • User ID column - Containing user ids in the format required by your identity provider (email addresses, logon names, or other identifiers). This column must be selected as the Identifier Column in your data access control.
    • One or more columns containing filter criteria (country or region names or ids, department names or ids, or any other criteria to control how your data is exposed to users). These columns must be selected as Criteria columns in your data access control.

 

  • Only users with the DW Space Administrator role (or equivalent privileges) can create data access controls in which criteria are defined as simple values. The organizations IT & Security Administrator Sarah should do this.

Click on the Security Icon on the left-hand bar. Click Roles.

 

Click on the DW Space Administrator role.

 

Click on the users Icon. Here the security administrator can assign or remove Users to the role.

 

Step 1: Setting up your tables.

Business users Anna and John oversee the management of the Permissions table, which is subsequently shared with the IT department. They have set up this table up in the Permissions Space.

Permissions Table Set up:

 

 

The IT & Security admin Sarah, who is the technical user, use the shared Permissions table as the basis for Data Access Controls.

Sales Products Table:

 

Step 3: Set up your Data Access Control in the IT Space.

 

Sarah logs into SAP Datasphere and navigates to the Data Access Control section.

In the side navigation area Sarah will click Data Access Controls, select the IT space, and click New Data Access Control to open the editor.

 

Select the input help and add the Permissions table to the ‘Permissions Entity’.

 

The permissions entity should include at least two columns:

One or more columns with filter criteria (e.g., Sales Org, Product) that determine how data is presented to users. These columns are known as Criteria columns in your data access control.

One column containing user IDs in the required format (such as email address). This column serves as the Identifier Column in your data access control.

 

 

Now Sarah can save and deploy the Data Access Control.

 

Step 4: Applying your DACs to a Graphical View

 

Sarah will create an analytical model in Datasphere, ensuring it respects the defined access controls.

Open the desired Graphical view. Below she has added the Sales Products Data table into a graphical view.

Click on the View Node. In the Model properties click on the plus icon to add your Data Access Control.

 

In the Join section map the columns from the Sales Org View to the Data Access Control in order the filter the data appropriately.

 

Change the Semantic Usage to Fact, turn Expose for Consumption On and add at least one Measure. This will allow you to create your Analytic Model which then Makes the object available for consumption in SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Now Sarah can Save and Deploy the View.

Now she can share this view with the Sales Space to be viewed by the Sales Team.

In the Sales Space users can create Analytical Models using the Graphical View as the source and users will be able to see data specific to their user ID.

 

Step 5: Consume your Model in SAP Analytic Cloud

Sarah ensures the analytical models are accessible in SAC.

Anna and John are pleased with the setup. They log into SAC and find the Sales Performance Dashboard tailored to their access levels. John sees aggregated sales data for the Irish Sales Department, while Anna has a comprehensive view of all sales data. The system ensures that sensitive data is secure and only accessible by authorized personnel.

Johns View in SAC:

 

Annas View in SAC:

 

Conclusion

In conclusion, setting up DAC’s in Datasphere is crucial to protect your sensitive information, regulate compliance, mitigate data breach risks, and improve operational efficiency. By following the outlined steps, users can ensure that their data assets are securely managed and accessed only by authorized personnel, thereby minimizing the potential for security incidents, and ensuring the integrity and confidentiality of their data throughout its lifecycle.

entry.title: Integrate SAP S/4HANA authorizations into SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Introduction

As more and more customers are leveraging the capabilities of SAP Datasphere with SAP S/4HANA, the role of authorisations is also becoming more important. With the newly released enhancement of Data Access Controls in SAP Datasphere there is an easier way to integrate authorizations than previously described in my previous blog post. This approach is valid for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise.

The goal is to provide a generic approach to integrate authorizations from SAP S/4HANA that allows you to implement row-level security with the necessary overview of what authorizations are assigned to which object.

The approach is based on the authorization objects from SAP S/4HANA and the idea is to reuse authorizations that are already maintained in SAP S/4HANA in SAP Datasphere for row-level security with Data Access Controls.

 

Prerequisites

A connection named “SAPS4H” to your SAP S/4HANA system with remote tables enabled must be created. The technical user must be authorized to access the following tables:

  1. AGR_USERS = user assignments to roles
  2. AGR_1251 = authorization values
  3. AGR_1252 = organizational element
  4. PUSER002 = assignment of mail address to user ID

The approach described in this blog post is a generic approach that can be used for various scenarios. To apply the generic scenario to a specific scenario you need to define which authorization object(s) are relevant for your case.

Example: you want to integrate authorizations for sales organizations, however there are many different authorisation objects related to the sales organisation (e. g. V_KONH_VKO, V_KNA_VKO, V_VBAK_VKO, …).

 

Preparation of Authorization Data

Based on the authorization data from SAP S/4HANA a view has to be created that performs the following processing steps:

  1. Restrict authorization values to the relevant authorization objects to reduce data volume
    (JOIN of AGR_1251 with local table SAP_CC_S4AUTH_REL_OBJECTS)
  2. Determine relevant authorization values between directly maintained values and organizational levels.
    (JOIN of AGR_1251 and AGR_1252 and calculated columns to derive the relevant values)
  3. Add role assignments to users from table AGR_USERS and also add mail addresses of users from table PUSER002
    (this way both user ID and mail addresses are available. Use the relevant one for your tenant).
  4. Derive the option column based on the maintained authorization values
    (derived formats are ALL, EQ, BT and CP)
  5. Hide not relevant columns and rename the columns to match the terminology in the Data Access Controls.

The output of the view looks like this:

This view will be the central integration path for all standard authorization concept based approaches and the basis for all Data Access Controls.

 

Creation of Data Access Controls

The next step is to create the required Data Access Controls. Instead of having one Data Access Control for many different scenarios, this approach uses different Data Access Controls for different scenarios (company code, sales organization, …).

You create the initial one and then only copy and adapt this Data Access Control.

In this example the first Data Access Control is based on the sales organization. You create a Data Access Control with structure “Operator and Values” and map the fields from the view:

(please map either USERNAME or SMTP_ADDR as Identifier, depending on the settings of your SAP Datasphere tenant. You are shown an exemplary identifier to understand which of the two is required).

You then select one of the criteria that are offered, in this case the V_VBAK_VKO for Sales Organisation. The idea is to have one Data Access Control per authorization object. However you are free to combine several into the same Data Access Control).

The second Data Access Control can be created by saving the Data Access Control under a new name and selecting a different criteria:

By separating the authorization scenarios into different Data Access Controls you gain a good overview of which scenario is applied to which view:

Alternatively you could keep all scenarios in one Data Access Control and then associate this generic Data Access Control to all relevant views. While you save the time to create the Data Acess Control, you do lose the easy overview on which scenario (“criterium” from the Data Access Control) is used for a view.

 

Summary

With this approach you can easily integrate authorizations from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere. It provides flexibility and is easy to use and should enable you to set up a concept quite easily.

And soon you will be able to import the required artefacts into you SAP Datasphere tenant as they will be released as SAP Community Content.

entry.title: Unlock the full potential of SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere have revolutionized the way how the traditional SAP analytics and data warehousing businesses operate. Compared to the classic BW and BusinessObjects, the SAP Business Technology Platform offer benefits that drive efficiency, scalability, and innovations.

" Sometimes the cloud applications are seen as too close for the customizations and very distant to the ad-hoc development, especially if it's compared to the traditional ABAP development or with the BO SDK."

This is not true!

This article is born by the experience on the field from the SAP Services Adoption Center, that supports everyday partners and customers to get the best out of the SAP Business Technology Platform; In this blog we are going to unveil some very important customization points that you should know while implementing projects for the enterpise. 

In the context of SAP BTP analytics and data management, hardcore development represents a way to solve complex requirements where there is no "out of the box" functionality available; developers wants to push the boundaries of technology to create robust, high-performance, and scalable solutions. This type of development is characterized by a relentless focus on optimizing code, deep understanding of the cloud infrastructure interaction, and the ability to solve complex problems under extreme constraints. Whether it's building a tailored UI components for SAP Analytics Cloud or developing interactive applications for SAP Datasphere, this blog will offer a good collection of use cases that you can easily reuse.

 

The following collection of advanced use cases is going to be regularly updated. Stay tuned !

  • SAP Datasphere News in May

 

 

Push SAP Analytics Cloud to the next levelPush SAP Datasphere to the next level

Realize your custom widget 

Now that Custom Widget is available with the Unified Story Experience since QRC/Q2 - 2023, and Widget Add-Ons since QRC/Q4 - 2023, we received a large amount of requests coming from SAC users searching for Samples in order to extend the Built-in set of visualisations in SAC.

  • SAP Public GitHub repository
  • Custom Widget Hands-on Guide
  • Custom Widget & Widget Add-Ons Samples Preview
  • Demystifying Custom Widgets for SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Diving into Custom Widgets with SAP Analytics Cloud

Boost the integration

SAP Analytics Cloud is extremely efficient when it's combinated with SAP Datasphere, here a collection of plug and play usecasaes

  • KPI-Dashboard with SAC and Datasphere
  • Extending SAC Planning - Acessing planning data with SAP Datasphere

Work with the API

The API allows third-party applications to access data stored on an SAP Analytics Cloud tenant. The API is best used for requesting small amounts of data in real time. You must have a tenant URL and SAP Analytics Cloud user. You must also configure OAuth to work with your application before you can use the REST API

  • Official guide from SAP Help
  • APIs: Getting Started Guide
  • Openness and flexibility with SAP Analytics Cloud’s APIs for data import and export ( Devtoberfest Sep 2023 )
  • Consume SAP APIs using oAuth and Node on GitHub code
  • Consume SAP APIs using oAuth and Hana XSA as  Community article

 

Use the power of Hana Cloud

  • Use the Datasphere data in BTP by sharing your space data 

 

The power of CLI

The SAP Datasphere Command Line Interface is a powerfull tool to interact with Dataphere not using the regular web interface but using your terminal. This approach gives you massive benefits in terms of rapidity and automations.

  • Official guide from SAP Help
  • Command-Line Interface for SAP Datasphere: Overview
  • Transformation Flow partition generation with SAP Datasphere CLI

Realize your own application & Integration

SAP Datasphere can be integrated with SAP Buid, SAP Build Code and SAP Business Application Studio to realize amazing applications on your data with read\write options.

  • Enable end-user data entry into SAP Datasphere via SAP Build Apps
  • Integrating with SAP Datasphere Consumption APIs using SAML Bearer Assertion

entry.title: Get Ready for a Data and AI Revolution: What's Hot at SAP SAPPHIRE 2024 - My personal picks!
entry.content:
Why SAP is Focusing on AI

SAP's strategic pivot towards AI is part of a comprehensive plan aimed at positioning the company for future growth. This initiative includes a significant investment in AI technologies to streamline operations and enhance efficiency. The creation of a dedicated AI growth area under the leadership of Dr. Philipp Herzig, SAP’s new Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer, underscores the centrality of AI in SAP’s future strategy. This move is designed to accelerate the development and deployment of AI solutions that can drive significant business value for SAP’s customers​ (SAP News Center)​​. You can find Dr. Herzig's session at SAPPHIRE here.

The Importance of SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere plays a crucial role in this AI-driven landscape. It represents a comprehensive data management solution that enables businesses to manage, integrate, and utilize their data more effectively. SAP Datasphere simplifies complex data environments by creating a unified data fabric, allowing organizations to access and leverage their data seamlessly. This capability is essential for maximizing the value of AI applications, which rely on high-quality, integrated data to function optimally​.

Datasphere's recent innovations, including the introduction of a knowledge graph and enhanced integration features, make it a pivotal tool for businesses looking to harness the power of data and AI. These advancements help companies transform their data into actionable insights, driving better decision-making and operational efficiency.

With close to 130 sessions at SAPPHIRE, the content can be a bit overwhelming. Below I've listed my top 10 sessions you should attend in my view.

Key Sessions on Data and AI

  1. Data in the Age of AI

    • Speakers: Irfan Khan, Dan Yu, Peter Howland
    • Overview: Discover how to maximize data value across SAP and third-party systems, with insights from Hershey's data transformation journey.
    • Session ID: BTP189

  2. Bringing AI from Prototype to Production

    • Speaker: Walter Sun
    • Overview: Learn about the journey of AI from prototype to production, focusing on identifying valuable AI applications and overcoming implementation challenges.
    • Session ID: TRE307

  3. Business Data Fabric: The Future of Data and AI

    • Speaker: Kaiser Larsen
    • Overview: Explore how SAP Datasphere simplifies data landscapes and empowers organizations.
    • Session ID: BTP151v

  4. SAP Datasphere: Looking Forward

    • Speaker: Torsten Ammon
    • Overview: Gain insights into new features and innovations in SAP Datasphere.
    • Session ID: BTP113

  5. Navigating the AI Era with SAP: Insights from the Executive Suite

    • Speakers: Andreas Welsch, Julia White, Michael Nilles, Walter Sun
    • Overview: Exclusive perspectives on the future of AI with SAP Business AI solutions.
    • Session ID: BAI220v

  6. Experience SAP Business AI in Action

    • Speaker: Richard Grandpierre
    • Overview: Demonstration of the Joule copilot and its impact on efficiency and decision-making.
    • Session ID: BAI900v

  7. Managing Your AI Risk: Responsible AI

    • Speaker: Dr. Bettina Laugwitz
    • Overview: Learn about mitigating AI bias and ensuring trust, privacy, and security.
    • Session ID: TRE312

  8. Learn About SAP's Responsible AI Practices

    • Speaker: Dr. Bettina Laugwitz
    • Overview: Understand SAP's commitment to ethical AI innovations.
    • Session ID: BAI206v

  9. Discover Recent and Upcoming AI Innovations for Business Functions

    • Speaker: Kai Muehlbauer
    • Overview: Learn about the Joule copilot AI Foundation and upcoming AI capabilities.
    • Session ID: BAI202

  10. Unlock the Potential of Your Data Assets: Data-Driven Decision-Making

    • Speaker: Frank Gundlich
    • Overview: Leverage technology and innovation to reshape decision-making processes.
    • Session ID: PAR309v

Conclusion

SAP SAPPHIRE 2024 is a great event for those interested in the latest advancements in Data and AI and SAP is going all in. With a diverse array of sessions, attendees will gain valuable insights into how these technologies can transform their businesses. Don't miss the opportunity to explore these sessions and stay ahead in the rapidly evolving landscape of Data and AI.

It's free, go for it.

For more information and to register for the event, visit the SAP SAPPHIRE 2024 event page and the virtual event page

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter June 2024
entry.content:
Dear customers,
Find here an overview of the SAP Enterprise Support Academy monthly newsletter on latest news, training courses, upcoming sessions, and further educational offerings.
The SAP Enterprise Support Academy provides expert guidance and high impact knowledge transfer services that enable you to innovate and be successful with your SAP solutions.

Subscribe to regularly receive latest news and stay informed about the wide range of educational offerings provided by the SAP Enterprise Support Academy that help you to build the digital skills tailored to your needs.

Preview available live sessions on SAP Support Portal, tailored per region:
APJ | EMEA | Latin America | North America*

Please note: A one-time registration for SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support [accessible here] is necessary to register for these events.

SAP Datasphere Connectivity Series

Good news! We’ve updated this series of E-Learnings on SAP Datasphere Connectivity with new assets on integration with Amazon S3 and Google BigQuery. Make deploying and integrating SAP Datasphere easier by completing these quick hit learnings!
Check out here: SAP Datasphere Connectivity Series

New! Discovery Center Mission for SAP Build Process Automation

Register for the first Discovery Center mission for SAP Enterprise Support for BTP. The mission helps you to get ramped-up and easily get started with SAP Build Process Automation.
Gain an overview and get started

New Dates in June: SAP Analytics Cloud EGI

Check out our recently refreshed content for the two virtual workshops on SAP Analytics Cloud BI and planning fundamentals. The sessions are multihour virtual sessions, delivered over 3-5 working days to you at no additional cost.
Save your seat and register through the blog post

All You Need to Know About SAP Cloud ALM

Join the EGI workshop and learn all about the benefits and capabilities of SAP Cloud ALM and to help you use its functionalities in your own environment. It combines Implementation, Operations, and Service topics to allow you to leverage the full potential of SAP Cloud ALM.

We have three sessions scheduled in June. Register here

Get Ready with SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA

Learn all about the SAP Readiness Check tool for SAP S/4HANA, and how it supports a successful conversion to SAP S/4HANA. We offer short and interactive live must-know sessions in multiple languages to get you prepared to start your SAP S/4HANA project.

Register here: SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA

Release Navigator for SAP Customer Experience: Q2 Update Available Now

We are excited to share that the Q2 updates to the release navigator for SAP Customer Experience had been automatically implemented, and include the most recent release enhancement, and information about future releases.

Access here

Unleashing the Power of Artificial Intelligence in Customer Support from SAP

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized the way businesses operate, opening doors to unprecedented opportunities for innovation and efficiency. Within the realm of customer support at SAP, these cutting-edge technologies play a crucial role in enhancing the customer experience and streamlining support processes.

Read more

SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network Seamlessly Integrated into SAP for Me

SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network are live on SAP for Me! The customer portal is your new central entry point to support, simplifying support-related tasks by using a set of digital self-service features to easily request support and resolve issues. SAP for Me is made for you!

Read more

Your Ultimate Guide for SAP Sapphire 2024 Orlando!

Gain valuable insights into innovative technologies and strategies that are shaping the future of customer support and business transformation. Mark your calendars for these must-attend sessions! See you soon at this year´s SAP Sapphire 2024 in Orlando from June 3-5.

Check it out!

Enhancing Support Experiences with Support Accreditation

Learn how Support Accreditation enhances the support experience for partners and customers. Get valuable insights on efficient problem resolution and how to interact with support teams for optimal results. The training incorporates the latest in AI and machine learning to prepare you for the future of support services. Don't miss out on leveraging these benefits and optimizing your service experience with SAP!

Read more in the interview

Success Story: LG Group

The LG Group is thankful to the SAP Mission Control Center team for their dedication and teamwork, which ensured the success of a critical event for LG. They appreciate the MCC team's support and look forward to continued collaboration for LG's future success.
